1.pericope_label: Dead to Sin, Alive to God
1.synthesis: Paul addresses the objection that grace should increase sin: 'Are we to continue in sin that grace may abound? By no means!' (Romans 6:1–2). He appeals to baptism: all who are baptized into Christ Jesus are baptized into his death; buried with him through baptism, so we might walk in newness of life (Romans 6:3–4). Our old self was crucified with him so the body of sin might be rendered powerless; one who has died has been freed from sin (Romans 6:6–7). The death Christ died, he died to sin once for all; the life he lives, he lives to God. So believers are to consider themselves dead to sin and alive to God in Christ Jesus (Romans 6:10–11). Sin must not reign; present yourselves to God as those alive from the dead—for you are not under law but under grace (Romans 6:12–14).
1.historical_context: The objection Paul addresses—'shall we go on sinning so grace may increase?'—was apparently a real accusation leveled against his gospel of free grace, or a logical implication some were drawing from his teaching (Romans 3:8). Baptism in the first century was not a private ceremony but a public, community-witnessed act of identification with Christ's death and resurrection—Paul's audience would have experienced it as a decisive boundary-crossing. The language of 'dying' and 'rising' with Christ is not merely metaphorical but sacramental: the believer's union with Christ in his death is real, not symbolic, and grounds the ethical imperative to live accordingly.
1.literary_structure: This chapter is the first ethical response to the theological argument of chs.1–5. The structure is indicative → imperative: what is true of believers in Christ (vv.1–11) grounds what they are to do (vv.12–14). The 'consider yourselves dead to sin and alive to God' of v.11 is the hinge—it calls believers to reckon as real what baptism enacted. The chapter moves from death-and-resurrection imagery to slave imagery in the second half, maintaining the pattern of corporate identification (dead/alive, slaves/freed) as the basis for ethical life.
1.theological_application: The indicative-imperative structure of this chapter is the grammar of Christian ethics: we do not strive to become what we are not yet, but to live in accordance with what we already are in Christ. Chrysostom recognized this as Paul's characteristic strategy: 'he continually digresseth into exhortation without making any twofold division' — doctrine and ethical imperative are inseparable throughout. Gill draws the practical logic of v.11: the believer is called to 'recognize and inhabit the reality that God has established: you are dead to sin's authority — live accordingly. The indicative grounds the imperative.' The Christian's struggle with sin is a real battle against a defeated power — which is precisely why the imperative to resist has traction and why Paul can ground the command 'let not sin reign' in the declarative fact that its legal claim has been discharged. The call to offer bodily members as 'instruments of righteousness' (v.13) — where hopla also means 'weapons' — is the holy-war reframing of what was formerly offered to sin.
1.christology: 'The death he died, he died to sin once for all; but the life he lives, he lives to God' (Romans 6:10) is Paul's foundational christological claim in the chapter: Christ's death was a comprehensive, unrepeatable event in which he exhausted sin's claim and power. The resurrection is the permanent 'alive to God' that follows—and believers' union with Christ means they share both. The 'once for all' (ephapax) of Christ's death is the theological ground of the believer's permanent freedom from sin's dominion: not a condition that must be re-achieved but a status established by his single historic act.

15.synthesis: Paul again preempts the objection: 'Are we to sin because we are not under law but under grace? By no means!' (Romans 6:15). He uses the slavery analogy: everyone is slave either to sin (leading to death) or to obedience (leading to righteousness); his readers were slaves to sin but became obedient from the heart to the teaching they received (Romans 6:16–17). Freed from sin, they have become slaves of righteousness (Romans 6:18). The end of sin-slavery was death; the end of righteousness-slavery is eternal life. For the wages of sin is death, but the free gift of God is eternal life in Christ Jesus our Lord (Romans 6:23).
15.historical_context: The slavery metaphor was not abstract for Paul's Roman audience—Rome's economy depended on slave labor, and the social reality of absolute master-slave loyalty was familiar to every Roman. Paul's use of slavery as a metaphor for moral allegiance exploits this familiarity: everyone serves a master, and the master determines the outcome of the service. The phrase 'from the heart' (v.17) is significant—the obedience that characterizes the new life is not external compliance but inward alignment. 'Wages of sin is death' vs. 'gift of God is eternal life' (v.23) is the letter's most memorable contrast: earned outcome versus gracious gift.
15.literary_structure: The second half of the chapter shifts the metaphor from death/life to slavery/freedom, but the underlying argument is identical: the status change that occurred in conversion has ontological consequences that demand ethical response. The rhetorical structure mirrors vv.1–14: objection → 'by no means!' → argument from the believer's actual status → ethical application. The climactic verse 23 synthesizes the entire chapter's argument: sin and grace operate in entirely different economic registers—one pays wages (deserved), the other gives gifts (undeserved)—and the outcomes differ correspondingly.
15.theological_application: 'The wages of sin is death, but the free gift of God is eternal life in Christ Jesus our Lord' (Romans 6:23) is perhaps the most compressed gospel statement in Romans. The contrast between 'wages' and 'gift' is theologically decisive: sin produces what it has earned; God gives what has not been earned. The church's proclamation must maintain this asymmetry—eternal life is not the reward for righteous living but the gift of a gracious God. At the same time, the slavery imagery shows that this gift creates a new allegiance: those who receive it become slaves of the one who gave it, and slavery to God produces righteousness rather than license.
15.christology: 'Eternal life in Christ Jesus our Lord' (Romans 6:23) is not a generic immortality but a specific participation in the risen Christ's own life—'alive to God in Christ Jesus' (v.11) is the same life he lives. The gift is the person: to receive eternal life is to be brought into living union with the one who conquered death. The 'Lord' (kyrios) at the verse's end is the title of the risen and exalted Christ—the one whose lordship is the alternative to sin's dominion. Allegiance has changed masters; the new master gives rather than demands and liberates rather than enslaves.
